segments.text:
Steps at which gene expression can be regulated
DNA
primary transcript
functional mRNA
polypeptide (primary translation product)
processed protein
active protein
transcription
RNA processing, nuclear export
(eukaryotes only)
translation
mRNA stability
protein processing
subunit assembly, cofactor interactions
protein targeting (eukaryotes)
protein modifications (activation/inhibition)
protein stability

Positive regulation of transcription
default state = little transcription initiation
requires activator or inducer to recruit RNA polymerase,
induce transcription of gene
in prokaryotes
“off”       no transcription
≠
minimal

Negative regulation of transcription
default state = gene is transcribed
requires repressor to block transcription initiation,
prevent transcription of gene
minimal

Positive regulation
Negative regulation
inducer is the activator, or it
causes activator to bind to DNA,
promoting transcription
inducer causes repressor to
stop binding to DNA and blocking
transcription; allows transcription

An operon – the basic idea
polycistronic mRNA

The lac operon
Jacob and Monod – Nobel prize, 1965
lactose induces transcription of operon

No lactose available:
Lactose available:
(allolactose)
Is this positive or negative regulation?

Allosteric protein = can change shape
lac repressor protein in its DNA binding conformation
use glucose first!

in cells, there is an inverse relationship between glucose levels and cAMP levels
cAMP
cyclic AMP
ATP
adenylate cyclase
glucose       cAMP
glucose       cAMP

cAMP binding protein
CAP (Catabolite Activator Protein) in prokaryotes
CRE (cAMP Response Element) Binding Protein in eukaryotes
must complex with cAMP to take on DNA binding conformation
binds to CRE = cAMP Response Element
transcriptional activator
recruits RNA pol; increases transcription

High glucose = low cAMP
Low glucose = high cAMP
Is this positive or negative regulation?
can’t bind DNA without cAMP

No lactose, high glucose – operon repressed; little transcription
No lactose, low glucose – operon still repressed; little transcription

High lactose, high glucose –operon not repressed; transcription allowed
High lactose, low glucose – operon induced; high levels of transcription

Mapping binding sites
results from DNase protection/DNA footprinting
repressor protein and RNA pol compete for same site on DNA
Protected by
cAMP-CAP

The ara operon – genes for the metabolism of arabinose
Regulators:
AraC protein – responds to arabinose
cAMP – indicator for glucose levels

AraC protein alone binds two sites in DNA, one distal
proteins interact
DNA looping
blocks RNA pol binding – low transcription
structural genes

AraC + arabinose = altered binding site preference
functions as inducer
Transcription further stimulated if also low glucose
